turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in case 22-166, Tyler v. Hennepin County. Hennepin County is where Minneapolis is. In 2010, Geraldine Tyler, who's now 94, moved from her condominium to an apartment building for seniors. She failed to pay the property taxes on the condo for three years. So Hennepin County seized it and sold it. The home sold for $40,000. Tyler owed only $15,000 in taxes and late fees. But the county kept the extra $25,000 for itself. Tyler sued claiming that the county had violated the Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment, which prohibits the government from taking a person's property without providing just compensation. The Eighth Circuit Court of Appeals dismissed her suit because Minnesota Law authorized the county to keep any surplus in the event of a tax sale. We granted certiorari to resolve this important constitutional question, which has divided courts around the country. Now Tyler acknowledges that she has to pay her property taxes and she agrees that the county could sell her condo to recover those unpaid property taxes. But she argues that the county cannot keep the extra value of her home beyond what she owed. We agree. The principle that the surplus from a tax sale of property belongs to the taxpayer goes back to the Magna Carta in 1215. It was the rule at common law in England and here and has always been the rule in a clear majority of the states. It was even the rule in Minnesota until they changed the law in 1935. That rule that any surplus goes to the taxpayer is also the rule in Minnesota when a home is sold because of a failure to pay a mortgage. The state just wants an exception for itself when it is the one taking real property in this sort of case. Minnesota says it can keep the surplus because the law in that state is that any surplus is not protected property. But we have long held that a government cannot change the rules about what counts as property just so it can avoid having to comply with the Takings Clause. That would make the clause a dead letter and we're not going to do that. The opinion of the Court is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Jackson joins.
